new file mode 100644
index 0000000..1ec5eb7
--- /dev/null
+++ b/CLAUDE.md
@@ -0,0 +1,34 @@
+# Project Guidelines
+
+## Architecture
+- This project follows the repository pattern for data access
+- Services depend on repositories, never on each other directly
+- Events are preferred over direct cross-service calls for decoupling
+- All business logic lives in the service layer
+
+## Coding Conventions
+- Use TypeScript strict mode — no `any` types
+- All public methods must have JSDoc comments
+- Use dependency injection via constructor parameters
+- Error handling: throw typed errors, never return null for errors
+- Prefer async/await over raw Promises
+
+## Testing
+- Tests use vitest
+- Each module has a corresponding test file in tests/
+- Mock external dependencies, test business logic directly
+- Minimum: test the happy path and one error path per public method
+
+## File Organization
+- src/models/ — Data models and interfaces
+- src/repositories/ — Data access layer (implements repository interfaces)
+- src/services/ — Business logic layer
+- src/events/ — Event definitions and event bus
+- src/utils/ — Shared utilities (database, logger, pagination)
+- src/config/ — Configuration files
+- tests/ — Test files mirroring src/ structure
+
+## Git Practices
+- Commit atomically per logical change
+- Write descriptive commit messages explaining the "why"
+- Run tests before committing

diff --git a/DESIGN.md b/DESIGN.md

index 0000000..60a99bb
--- /dev/null
+++ b/DESIGN.md
@@ -0,0 +1,207 @@
+# Analytics Dashboard API — Design Document
+
+## Overview
+
+This document describes the design of the analytics dashboard API added to
+TaskFlow Pro. The API provides aggregated views of order and user data for a
+frontend dashboard. **This is a scaffold** — the service methods return
+placeholder data; subsequent sessions will implement real aggregation logic.
+
+---
+
+## Route Structure
+
+| Method | Path | Handler | Description |
+|--------|------|---------|-------------|
+| `GET` | `/api/analytics/dashboard` | `handleGetDashboard` | Full dashboard snapshot (all widgets in one call) |
+| `GET` | `/api/analytics/metrics/:metricType` | `handleGetMetric` | Detailed view of a single metric with time series |
+
+### Why two routes?
+
+- **Dashboard route** — serves the initial page load. Returns every widget's
+  data in a single response to avoid a waterfall of requests from the frontend.
+- **Metric detail route** — lets the user drill into a specific metric with
+  different date ranges or granularities without re-fetching the entire
+  dashboard.
+
+### Common query parameters
+
+All endpoints accept:
+
+| Param | Type | Required | Default | Example |
+|-------|------|----------|---------|---------|
+| `startDate` | ISO-8601 string | yes | — | `2026-01-01` |
+| `endDate` | ISO-8601 string | yes | — | `2026-03-31` |
+| `granularity` | `hour \| day \| week \| month` | no | `day` | `week` |
+
+The metric detail route additionally requires `:metricType` as a path
+parameter (`revenue`, `orders`, `users`, `average-order-value`).
+
+---
+
+## Data Models (`src/models/analytics.ts`)
+
+### Query types
+- **`AnalyticsQuery`** — base query with date range and optional granularity.
+- **`MetricsQuery`** — extends `AnalyticsQuery` with a `metricType` field.
+
+### Response types
+- **`TimeSeriesPoint`** — `{ timestamp, value, label? }` — a single bucket in
+  a time-series chart.
+- **`MetricSummary`** — current value, previous-period value, percent change,
+  and trend direction. Powers the summary cards at the top of the dashboard.
+- **`OrderStatusBreakdown`** — `{ status, count, percent }` — for a donut/pie
+  chart of order statuses.
+- **`TopUser`** — `{ userId, userName, totalOrders, totalRevenue }` — for a
+  leaderboard widget.
+- **`DashboardData`** — composite type assembling all of the above into one
+  payload.
+- **`MetricResponse`** — summary + time series for the single-metric endpoint.
+
+### Design rationale
+- Types are **pure interfaces** with no class baggage, following the existing
+  model pattern (`src/models/order.ts`, `src/models/user.ts`).
+- `MetricType` is a string-literal union so new metrics can be added without
+  touching the aggregation framework — just add a case to the switch.
+- Dates are `Date` objects in the domain layer; the route layer handles
+  string parsing.
+
+---
+
+## Service Layer (`src/services/analytics.service.ts`)
+
+### Class: `AnalyticsService`
+
+**Dependencies** (constructor-injected, following existing pattern):
+- `OrderRepository` — read-only access to orders.
+- `UserRepository` — read-only access to users.
+
+**Public methods:**
+| Method | Returns | Used by |
+|--------|---------|---------|
+| `getDashboard(query)` | `DashboardData` | Dashboard route |
+| `getMetric(query)` | `MetricResponse` | Metric detail route |
+
+**Private helpers (stubbed — implement next):**
+| Method | Purpose |
+|--------|---------|
+| `getAllMetricSummaries` | Computes summary cards for all metric types |
+| `computeMetricSummary` | Computes a single `MetricSummary` |
+| `getTimeSeries` | Builds bucketed time-series data |
+| `getOrderStatusBreakdown` | Groups orders by status |
+| `getTopUsers` | Ranks users by revenue |
+
+### Implementation guide for the next developer
+
+1. **Time bucketing** — Write a helper `generateBuckets(start, end, granularity)`
+   that returns `{ bucketStart: Date, bucketEnd: Date }[]`. Use this in both
+   `getTimeSeries` and anywhere you need date-range iteration.
+
+2. **Previous-period comparison** — For `MetricSummary.changePercent`, compute
+   the previous period by shifting the query window backwards by its own
+   length: `previousStart = startDate - (endDate - startDate)`.
+
+3. **Filtering by date range** — `OrderRepository.findAll()` accepts a
+   predicate. Filter with:
+   ```ts
+   orders.filter(o => o.createdAt >= query.startDate && o.createdAt <= query.endDate)
+   ```
+
+4. **Trend calculation:**
+   ```ts
+   const change = prev === 0 ? 0 : ((curr - prev) / prev) * 100;
+   const trend = change > 0 ? 'up' : change < 0 ? 'down' : 'flat';
+   ```
+
+5. **Top users** — Group orders by `userId`, sum `total`, count orders, join
+   with `UserRepository.findById()` for the name, sort descending, take N.
+
+---
+
+## Route Layer (`src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`)
+
+### Framework-agnostic handlers
+
+This codebase has **no HTTP framework dependency**. The route layer provides:
+
+- **`analyticsRoutes`** — a static array of `RouteDefinition` objects
+  (`{ method, path, description }`) that a framework adapter can iterate over
+  to register routes.
+- **`parseAnalyticsQuery` / `parseMetricType`** — validation functions that
+  convert raw string params into typed domain objects (or throw descriptive
+  errors for 400 responses).
+- **`handleGetDashboard` / `handleGetMetric`** — pure async functions that
+  accept an `AnalyticsService` instance and raw params, returning typed
+  response payloads.
+
+### Integrating with a framework
+
+Example Express adapter (not included — write when Express is added):
+```ts
+import express from 'express';
+import { analyticsRoutes, handleGetDashboard } from './routes/analytics.routes.js';
+
+const router = express.Router();
+router.get('/api/analytics/dashboard', async (req, res) => {
+  try {
+    const data = await handleGetDashboard(analyticsService, req.query);
+    res.json(data);
+  } catch (err) {
+    res.status(400).json({ error: err.message });
+  }
+});
+```
+
+---
+
+## Architectural Decisions
+
+### 1. Single dashboard endpoint vs. one endpoint per widget
+**Chose:** Single composite endpoint.
+**Why:** Reduces frontend round-trips. The data is cheap to compute (in-memory
+store) and the payload is small. If performance becomes an issue later,
+individual widget endpoints already exist as private methods — promoting them
+to public + adding routes is straightforward.
+
+### 2. Framework-agnostic route layer
+**Chose:** Plain functions + route definition array, no Express/Fastify dep.
+**Why:** The existing codebase is a framework-free library. Adding an HTTP
+dependency would be a larger architectural change that should be a separate
+decision. The handler functions are testable without spinning up a server.
+
+### 3. Read-only service (no new repository)
+**Chose:** AnalyticsService reads from existing OrderRepository and
+UserRepository; no dedicated AnalyticsRepository.
+**Why:** Analytics are derived views over existing data. Adding a separate
+repository would mean duplicating data or maintaining a materialised view,
+which is premature for an in-memory store. If a persistent DB with
+aggregation queries is added, reconsider.
+
+### 4. Stub implementations returning placeholder data
+**Chose:** Methods return empty/zeroed data rather than throwing "not
+implemented".
+**Why:** Allows the scaffolded code to compile, pass type checks, and be
+called from tests without error. The next session fills in real logic.
+
+---
+
+## File Map
+
+```
+src/
+  models/analytics.ts          ← All analytics types and interfaces
+  services/analytics.service.ts ← Aggregation logic (stubbed)
+  routes/analytics.routes.ts    ← Route definitions + handlers
+  index.ts                      ← Updated barrel exports
+```
+
+---
+
+## Open Items for Next Sessions
+
+- [ ] Implement real aggregation in `AnalyticsService` private methods
+- [ ] Add unit tests in `tests/services/analytics.service.test.ts`
+- [ ] Add validation tests for `parseAnalyticsQuery` / `parseMetricType`
+- [ ] Consider caching dashboard results if computation becomes expensive
+- [ ] Add analytics-related events to `event-types.ts` if dashboard views
+      need to be tracked

diff --git a/src/services/analytics.service.ts b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..0242fc4

+++ b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,218 @@
+/**
+ * Analytics service — aggregation logic for the analytics dashboard.
+ *
+ * Depends on:
+ * - OrderRepository  (read-only access to order data)
+ * - UserRepository   (read-only access to user data)
+ *
+ * Architecture notes:
+ * - This service is READ-ONLY; it never mutates orders or users.
+ * - Every public method accepts an AnalyticsQuery (date range + granularity)
+ *   so that callers control the window of data being analysed.
+ * - The route handler layer (analytics.routes.ts) is responsible for parsing
+ *   HTTP query-string params into the typed query objects.
+ * - Methods are intentionally stubbed with placeholder data. The next session
+ *   should replace each stub with real aggregation against the repositories.
+ *
+ * Implementation guide for the next developer:
+ * 1. getDashboard() should call the other methods internally and assemble
+ *    the full DashboardData payload.
+ * 2. Time-series helpers should bucket data by the requested granularity.
+ *    Consider a private helper: bucketByGranularity(dates, granularity).
+ * 3. MetricSummary.changePercent compares the queried period with an
+ *    equally-sized preceding period (e.g. "last 7 days vs previous 7 days").
+ * 4. Keep aggregation in-memory for now (the Database is an in-memory store).
+ *    If a persistent store is added later, push aggregation to query layer.
+ */
+
+import type { OrderRepository } from '../repositories/order.repository.js';
+import type { UserRepository } from '../repositories/user.repository.js';
+import type {
+  AnalyticsQuery,
+  DashboardData,
+  MetricResponse,
+  MetricSummary,
+  MetricType,
+  MetricsQuery,
+  OrderStatusBreakdown,
+  TimeGranularity,
+  TimeSeriesPoint,
+  TopUser,
+} from '../models/analytics.js';
+import { Logger } from '../utils/logger.js';
+
+export class AnalyticsService {
+  private logger = new Logger('AnalyticsService');
+
+  constructor(
+    private orderRepository: OrderRepository,
+    private userRepository: UserRepository,
+  ) {}
+
+  /**
+   * Return the full dashboard payload.
+   *
+   * This is the primary entry point for GET /api/analytics/dashboard.
+   * It assembles every widget's data in one call so the frontend can
+   * render without multiple round-trips.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace placeholder data with real aggregation.
+   */
+  async getDashboard(query: AnalyticsQuery): Promise<DashboardData> {
+    this.logger.info(
+      `Building dashboard: ${query.startDate.toISOString()} – ${query.endDate.toISOString()}`,
+    );
+
+    const granularity = query.granularity ?? 'day';
+
+    const [
+      metrics,
+      revenueTimeSeries,
+      ordersTimeSeries,
+      orderStatusBreakdown,
+      topUsers,
+    ] = await Promise.all([
+      this.getAllMetricSummaries(query),
+      this.getTimeSeries({ ...query, metricType: 'revenue', granularity }),
+      this.getTimeSeries({ ...query, metricType: 'orders', granularity }),
+      this.getOrderStatusBreakdown(query),
+      this.getTopUsers(query),
+    ]);
+
+    return {
+      metrics,
+      revenueTimeSeries,
+      ordersTimeSeries,
+      orderStatusBreakdown,
+      topUsers,
+      generatedAt: new Date(),
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Return summary + time series for a single metric.
+   *
+   * This is the entry point for GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace placeholder data with real aggregation.
+   */
+  async getMetric(query: MetricsQuery): Promise<MetricResponse> {
+    this.logger.info(`Fetching metric: ${query.metricType}`);
+
+    const granularity = query.granularity ?? 'day';
+    const summary = await this.computeMetricSummary(query.metricType, query);
+    const timeSeries = await this.getTimeSeries({ ...query, granularity });
+
+    return {
+      summary,
+      timeSeries,
+      query: {
+        metricType: query.metricType,
+        startDate: query.startDate,
+        endDate: query.endDate,
+        granularity,
+      },
+    };
+  }
+
+  // ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+  // Internal aggregation methods (stubs — implement in next session)
+  // ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+  /**
+   * Compute summaries for all standard metrics.
+   *
+   * Implementation: iterate over MetricType literals and call
+   * computeMetricSummary for each.
+   */
+  private async getAllMetricSummaries(
+    query: AnalyticsQuery,
+  ): Promise<MetricSummary[]> {
+    const types: MetricType[] = ['revenue', 'orders', 'users', 'average-order-value'];
+    return Promise.all(types.map((t) => this.computeMetricSummary(t, query)));
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Compute a single MetricSummary.
+   *
+   * Implementation guide:
+   * - revenue:             sum of order.total within the date range
+   * - orders:              count of orders within the date range
+   * - users:               count of users created within the date range
+   * - average-order-value: revenue / orders
+   *
+   * For the "previous" period, shift startDate and endDate backwards by
+   * (endDate - startDate) and repeat the query.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace stub with real aggregation.
+   */
+  private async computeMetricSummary(
+    metricType: MetricType,
+    query: AnalyticsQuery,
+  ): Promise<MetricSummary> {
+    // Stub — returns zeroed placeholder
+    return {
+      metricType,
+      currentValue: 0,
+      previousValue: 0,
+      changePercent: 0,
+      trend: 'flat',
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Build a time series for the given metric and granularity.
+   *
+   * Implementation guide:
+   * 1. Generate time buckets from startDate to endDate using granularity.
+   * 2. For each bucket, aggregate the metric value from repository data.
+   * 3. Return an array of TimeSeriesPoint.
+   *
+   * Consider extracting a private `generateBuckets(start, end, granularity)`
+   * helper that returns an array of { bucketStart, bucketEnd } objects.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace stub with real aggregation.
+   */
+  private async getTimeSeries(
+    query: MetricsQuery,
+  ): Promise<TimeSeriesPoint[]> {
+    // Stub — returns empty series
+    return [];
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Count orders grouped by status for the queried date range.
+   *
+   * Implementation guide:
+   * 1. Fetch all orders within startDate–endDate.
+   * 2. Group by order.status.
+   * 3. Compute count and percent for each group.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace stub with real aggregation.
+   */
+  private async getOrderStatusBreakdown(
+    query: AnalyticsQuery,
+  ): Promise<OrderStatusBreakdown[]> {
+    // Stub — returns empty breakdown
+    return [];
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Return the top N users by revenue in the queried date range.
+   *
+   * Implementation guide:
+   * 1. Fetch all orders within startDate–endDate.
+   * 2. Group by userId, summing order.total and counting orders.
+   * 3. Join with UserRepository to get userName.
+   * 4. Sort descending by totalRevenue, take top 10.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace stub with real aggregation.
+   */
+  private async getTopUsers(
+    query: AnalyticsQuery,
+    limit: number = 10,
+  ): Promise<TopUser[]> {
+    // Stub — returns empty list
+    return [];
+  }
+}

## Codebase Orientation
This is a TypeScript service (~1,140 lines, ~24 files). You'll be adding a new analytics feature. Follow existing patterns:
- `src/services/` — existing service classes (follow this pattern for your analytics service)
- `src/models/` — existing model/type definitions (put analytics types here)
- `src/routes/` or `src/controllers/` — existing route handlers (if present, follow their pattern)
- `tests/` — existing test files

Directory structure:
```
src/
  models/        # Data models
  services/      # Business logic
  repositories/  # Data access layer
  utils/         # Shared utilities
tests/           # Test files
```

Your task: Design and scaffold the API for a new analytics dashboard. Create the route structure, data models, and write a brief design doc.

Specifically:
1. Design the analytics dashboard API: decide on routes (e.g., GET /api/analytics/dashboard, GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type), request/response shapes, and data flow.
2. Create TypeScript interfaces/types for analytics data models (e.g., DashboardData, MetricSummary, TimeSeriesPoint) in `src/models/` or `src/types/`.
3. Scaffold the route files with stub handlers that return placeholder data.
4. Write a brief design document (DESIGN.md or inline comments) explaining:
   - The route structure and why
   - The data model design
   - How the aggregation service should work (for the next developer)
   - Any architectural decisions you made
5. Make sure the scaffolded code compiles.

Important:
- Focus on DESIGN over implementation — the next sessions will implement the actual logic.
- Create clear interfaces that downstream sessions can implement against.
- Document your decisions thoroughly — 4 more developers will build on your foundation.

parameters.content:
# Analytics Dashboard API — Design Document

## Overview

This document describes the design of the analytics dashboard API added to
TaskFlow Pro. The API provides aggregated views of order and user data for a
frontend dashboard. **This is a scaffold** — the service methods return
placeholder data; subsequent sessions will implement real aggregation logic.

---

## Route Structure

| Method | Path | Handler | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `GET` | `/api/analytics/dashboard` | `handleGetDashboard` | Full dashboard snapshot (all widgets in one call) |
| `GET` | `/api/analytics/metrics/:metricType` | `handleGetMetric` | Detailed view of a single metric with time series |

### Why two routes?

- **Dashboard route** — serves the initial page load. Returns every widget's
  data in a single response to avoid a waterfall of requests from the frontend.
- **Metric detail route** — lets the user drill into a specific metric with
  different date ranges or granularities without re-fetching the entire
  dashboard.

### Common query parameters

All endpoints accept:

| Param | Type | Required | Default | Example |
|-------|------|----------|---------|---------|
| `startDate` | ISO-8601 string | yes | — | `2026-01-01` |
| `endDate` | ISO-8601 string | yes | — | `2026-03-31` |
| `granularity` | `hour \| day \| week \| month` | no | `day` | `week` |

The metric detail route additionally requires `:metricType` as a path
parameter (`revenue`, `orders`, `users`, `average-order-value`).

---

## Data Models (`src/models/analytics.ts`)

### Query types
- **`AnalyticsQuery`** — base query with date range and optional granularity.
- **`MetricsQuery`** — extends `AnalyticsQuery` with a `metricType` field.

### Response types
- **`TimeSeriesPoint`** — `{ timestamp, value, label? }` — a single bucket in
  a time-series chart.
- **`MetricSummary`** — current value, previous-period value, percent change,
  and trend direction. Powers the summary cards at the top of the dashboard.
- **`OrderStatusBreakdown`** — `{ status, count, percent }` — for a donut/pie
  chart of order statuses.
- **`TopUser`** — `{ userId, userName, totalOrders, totalRevenue }` — for a
  leaderboard widget.
- **`DashboardData`** — composite type assembling all of the above into one
  payload.
- **`MetricResponse`** — summary + time series for the single-metric endpoint.

### Design rationale
- Types are **pure interfaces** with no class baggage, following the existing
  model pattern (`src/models/order.ts`, `src/models/user.ts`).
- `MetricType` is a string-literal union so new metrics can be added without
  touching the aggregation framework — just add a case to the switch.
- Dates are `Date` objects in the domain layer; the route layer handles
  string parsing.

---

## Service Layer (`src/services/analytics.service.ts`)

### Class: `AnalyticsService`

**Dependencies** (constructor-injected, following existing pattern):
- `OrderRepository` — read-only access to orders.
- `UserRepository` — read-only access to users.

**Public methods:**
| Method | Returns | Used by |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `getDashboard(query)` | `DashboardData` | Dashboard route |
| `getMetric(query)` | `MetricResponse` | Metric detail route |

**Private helpers (stubbed — implement next):**
| Method | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `getAllMetricSummaries` | Computes summary cards for all metric types |
| `computeMetricSummary` | Computes a single `MetricSummary` |
| `getTimeSeries` | Builds bucketed time-series data |
| `getOrderStatusBreakdown` | Groups orders by status |
| `getTopUsers` | Ranks users by revenue |

### Implementation guide for the next developer

1. **Time bucketing** — Write a helper `generateBuckets(start, end, granularity)`
   that returns `{ bucketStart: Date, bucketEnd: Date }[]`. Use this in both
   `getTimeSeries` and anywhere you need date-range iteration.

2. **Previous-period comparison** — For `MetricSummary.changePercent`, compute
   the previous period by shifting the query window backwards by its own
   length: `previousStart = startDate - (endDate - startDate)`.

3. **Filtering by date range** — `OrderRepository.findAll()` accepts a
   predicate. Filter with:
   ```ts
   orders.filter(o => o.createdAt >= query.startDate && o.createdAt <= query.endDate)
   ```

4. **Trend calculation:**
   ```ts
   const change = prev === 0 ? 0 : ((curr - prev) / prev) * 100;
   const trend = change > 0 ? 'up' : change < 0 ? 'down' : 'flat';
   ```

5. **Top users** — Group orders by `userId`, sum `total`, count orders, join
   with `UserRepository.findById()` for the name, sort descending, take N.

---

## Route Layer (`src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`)

### Framework-agnostic handlers

This codebase has **no HTTP framework dependency**. The route layer provides:

- **`analyticsRoutes`** — a static array of `RouteDefinition` objects
  (`{ method, path, description }`) that a framework adapter can iterate over
  to register routes.
- **`parseAnalyticsQuery` / `parseMetricType`** — validation functions that
  convert raw string params into typed domain objects (or throw descriptive
  errors for 400 responses).
- **`handleGetDashboard` / `handleGetMetric`** — pure async functions that
  accept an `AnalyticsService` instance and raw params, returning typed
  response payloads.

### Integrating with a framework

Example Express adapter (not included — write when Express is added):
```ts
import express from 'express';
import { analyticsRoutes, handleGetDashboard } from './routes/analytics.routes.js';

const router = express.Router();
router.get('/api/analytics/dashboard', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const data = await handleGetDashboard(analyticsService, req.query);
    res.json(data);
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(400).json({ error: err.message });
  }
});
```

---

## Architectural Decisions

### 1. Single dashboard endpoint vs. one endpoint per widget
**Chose:** Single composite endpoint.
**Why:** Reduces frontend round-trips. The data is cheap to compute (in-memory
store) and the payload is small. If performance becomes an issue later,
individual widget endpoints already exist as private methods — promoting them
to public + adding routes is straightforward.

### 2. Framework-agnostic route layer
**Chose:** Plain functions + route definition array, no Express/Fastify dep.
**Why:** The existing codebase is a framework-free library. Adding an HTTP
dependency would be a larger architectural change that should be a separate
decision. The handler functions are testable without spinning up a server.

### 3. Read-only service (no new repository)
**Chose:** AnalyticsService reads from existing OrderRepository and
UserRepository; no dedicated AnalyticsRepository.
**Why:** Analytics are derived views over existing data. Adding a separate
repository would mean duplicating data or maintaining a materialised view,
which is premature for an in-memory store. If a persistent DB with
aggregation queries is added, reconsider.

### 4. Stub implementations returning placeholder data
**Chose:** Methods return empty/zeroed data rather than throwing "not
implemented".
**Why:** Allows the scaffolded code to compile, pass type checks, and be
called from tests without error. The next session fills in real logic.

---

## File Map

```
src/
  models/analytics.ts          ← All analytics types and interfaces
  services/analytics.service.ts ← Aggregation logic (stubbed)
  routes/analytics.routes.ts    ← Route definitions + handlers
  index.ts                      ← Updated barrel exports
```

---

## Open Items for Next Sessions

- [ ] Implement real aggregation in `AnalyticsService` private methods
- [ ] Add unit tests in `tests/services/analytics.service.test.ts`
- [ ] Add validation tests for `parseAnalyticsQuery` / `parseMetricType`
- [ ] Consider caching dashboard results if computation becomes expensive
- [ ] Add analytics-related events to `event-types.ts` if dashboard views
      need to be tracked

alternatives.option: Add Express as a dependency and create real route handlers
alternatives.reason_rejected: Would be a larger architectural change affecting the whole project, not scoped to analytics

alternatives.option: Skip route layer entirely, only provide service
alternatives.reason_rejected: Downstream developers would have to write validation and parsing logic themselves with no guidance

parameters.context: The existing codebase is a framework-free TypeScript library with zero runtime dependencies. Analytics needs an HTTP-facing route layer.
parameters.rationale: Adding Express/Fastify would be a large architectural change beyond the analytics scope. Plain functions are testable without a server, and a thin adapter can be written when a framework is chosen.
parameters.summary: Analytics API uses framework-agnostic route handlers (plain async functions) instead of adding an HTTP framework dependency

alternatives.option: Create a dedicated AnalyticsRepository with pre-computed aggregates
alternatives.reason_rejected: Premature optimization — duplicates data in an in-memory store with no performance need

assumptions: Analytics query volume is low enough for on-the-fly aggregation
parameters.context: Analytics data is derived from existing orders and users. Could create a separate repository or materialised views.
parameters.rationale: Analytics are computed views over existing data. The in-memory Database makes separate storage unnecessary. If a persistent DB with native aggregation is added, reconsider.
parameters.summary: AnalyticsService reads from existing OrderRepository and UserRepository — no dedicated analytics repository

alternatives.option: Separate endpoint per widget (metrics, timeseries, breakdown, topusers)
alternatives.reason_rejected: Creates a waterfall of requests on page load with no caching layer to mitigate it

parameters.context: Dashboard UI needs metrics, time series, status breakdown, and top users. Could serve each widget separately or as one payload.
parameters.rationale: Reduces frontend round-trips. Payload is small for in-memory data. Individual widget methods exist as private helpers and can be promoted to public endpoints later if needed.
parameters.summary: Single composite GET /api/analytics/dashboard endpoint returns all widget data in one response
